[1/6] The #jobsearch is begging to be disrupted. My team - https://t.co/4h6a89fiIY - is building something 10x better than current career tools that'll actually help *anyone* put their best self forward. Here are some of the problems we're going to solve:

🧵

[2/6] 1) Lots of #careertools that help you “upgrade your #resume” aren't worth squat if your materials don't reflect the best of who you are in the first place. Garbage in, garbage out! The solution isn't a better editor, it's something that *facilitates introspection*.

[3/6] 2) Also, in the #ChatGPT era, it's easier than ever to buff (or even fake) your #resume/#coverletter, but what's the point if you can't *actually* talk about what sets you apart in an interview? Feels like progress in the short term, pretty much self-defeating in the long run

[4/6] 3) Meanwhile, #jobboards suck to be on. Who needs another #disruptive unicorn that's ultimately still filtering people by degree and school? To do better, you need to build from the ground up

[5/6] 4) We know #Introspection is hard. Doing it by yourself is even harder. Pressure to “just #apply” means people don't think about what they want or why they're good for a role. It also means they submit the same rez and CL for most roles. Sadly, that just doesn't cut it.

[6/6] Introspection and personalized storytelling are at the heart of The ApplyAI. Sign up for our waitlist at https://t.co/4h6a89fiIY. First 100 people get a free month upoen release + personalized onboarding. 75 slots remaining y'all!

[1/5] These cuts are neither unique nor surprising. 

The AI genie is out of the bottle, and information-centric responsibilities and the workers that perform them will be the first on the chopping block.

https://t.co/YAtLt8Z8Yu

[2/5] LLMs - in my experience - are extremely efficient when given the right prompts and well-scoped-out data

Unfortunately for white-collar workers, LLMs can make a single well-trained person just as effective as several non-AI-enabled individuals.

[3/5] The good news? AI can't match up to your soft skills. 

You need to proactively find and cultivate your competitive advantage. 

Ironically, in a world full of AI, that's usually how well you work with other humans.

[4/5] For many of us, that means finding ways to contribute to our teams/the bottom line in ways that go beyond our literal output on Excel or PowerPoint (and I say that as an ex-consultant).

[5/5] Can you decompose a difficult problem into digestable chunks? 

Can you help your teammates do their job better with smart interventions?

These value-adds may not be what you were initially hired for. Unfortunately, they're going to matter far more in the long run.

[1/5] @jordanglee With pleasure @jordanglee!

Basically, The SAT is too easy at the top.

High scores are common enough that they don't differentiate. 

So admissions looks elsewhere—essays, extracurriculars, "leadership." All of which favor kids with resources and coaching.

[2/5] @jordanglee A harder test creates headroom.

Right now there's a ceiling effect. Raise the ceiling, and you get real stratification at the top. The signal becomes useful again.

[3/5] @jordanglee "But won't that hurt disadvantaged students?"

No. A harder test + free, abundant prep resources is MORE equitable than an easy test with expensive coaching.

The prep is trainable. Make it free. Make the test hard enough that coaching alone can't close the gap.

[4/5] @jordanglee Also: diversify what's being measured.

SAT tests a narrow slice—math and verbal reasoning. But cognitive capability is wider than that.

Spatial reasoning. Pattern recognition. Problem decomposition. Working memory under load.

One test, broader signal. Harder to coach because…

[5/5] @jordanglee Now here's where it gets interesting: AI-based adaptive testing.

Imagine tests that generate novel problems on the fly. No question banks to memorize. No patterns to recognize from last year's exam.

You can't prep for questions that don't exist yet. You can only build genuine…

Don't you think the incumbent should attend a debate regardless if his or her primary opponents have a sizeable slice of voter support? Believe me when I say that I would rather have Biden than the alternatives on both sides, but I also think it's undemocratic to stifle challengers to this extent

That's all well and good and I agree that debating nutjobs like RFK is like mud-wrestling a pig, but imo the debates aren't sanctioned because dems don't think Biden can handle it. If we had the Biden that crushed Paul Ryan around, debates would be held. Not holding the DNC accountable for favoring certain candidate through undemocratic means is precisely why we're all lowkey panicking going into 2024.

1. Past precedent is separate from the principle of the matter unless you're arguing that we should always do things like the way they've been done just because.

2. Per politico (https://t.co/ku8LnJ2y73) the rules have been 1 percent in three major polls as recently as 2020. I believe both RFK and MW clear that bar.

I think in a democratic process, if you have challengers that meet established thresholds for debate consideration, then you should debate them regardless of their beliefs. If you feel like that would be beneath Biden or would turn the stage into a freakshow, then I don't think I can persuade you and that's fine. I appreciate you engaging in the discussion regardless.

I will admit that I'm less concerned with norms than what I believe is the right way to run the process as well as the fact that my position would indeed require Biden to be more accomodating than incumbents in the past have been. You're absolutely correct in those regards, and even so, I believe that not doing that is undemocratic. Imo the essence of democracy is engaging with all opinions, not just the ones that I personally deem as worthy and no matter how irritating or idiotic I find those positions to be. I nonetheless appreciate you taking the time to respond. I generally admire your takes, Andrew, and it's nice to go back and forth with you on a substantive topic.

@ryancarson is correct

The future belongs to the professional "superstar" who will deliver 10x the value (and demand at least 2-3x the comp to do so)

The question then becomes, how do you find these people?

I dig into this—and more—in my newest medium post

https://t.co/rXdJ1RL2gy

Surely one of the more verifiable aspects  of society's response to technological progress is a reset in expectations-- what was once considered "human work" may quickly exist outside of consideration for comparison. 

Speaking as someone that prided himself on his writing skills, I definitely felt existential dread at first seeing AI rapidly produce (relatively) high quality writing in seconds. 

Over time, though, the act of everyday writing has become commoditized to the point where I'm willing to surrender the artisanal quality of my writing for expedience's sake. 

Note that this stance only applies for professional work--in sports and art, things are admittedly going to be less clear-cut, but I think we will eventually come to psychologically distinguish the valence of a task completed by an AI/robot and a human being.

As standardized prep teacher who scored in the 99th percentile in everything SAT to the GMAT:

 1. Bring back the test and make it harder * [we need better stratification in the top bands]

|2. Make a basic level of training materials (including unlimited diagnostic tests) *AND* AI-based trainers free for all <$200K households

|3. First official test is on the house

* there's a lot of nuance to "harder" that I can elaborate on if there's interest

This will tackle 80% of the equity and meritocracy concerns that people have

Oh we absolutely are. In theory, the whole idea of holistic admissions was that other parts of the qualifying process would serve as signaling for the traits you're describing. As we know, that is not necessarily the case at all schools and in all cases. 

Fwiw i actually think that we need a new generation of in situ, dynamic assessments to serve that purpose that are required or available in parallel

Also: diversify what's being measured.

SAT tests a narrow slice—math and verbal reasoning. But cognitive capability is wider than that.

Spatial reasoning. Pattern recognition. Problem decomposition. Working memory under load.

One test, broader signal. Harder to coach because you can't specialize.

Now here's where it gets interesting: AI-based adaptive testing.

Imagine tests that generate novel problems on the fly. No question banks to memorize. No patterns to recognize from last year's exam.

You can't prep for questions that don't exist yet. You can only build genuine reasoning capability.

That's the endgame.
